coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted exfoliation and tearing of graphene oxide in the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Rasuli, Hadi
Rasuli, Reza
Alizadeh, Mahdi
BoonTong, Goh
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we present a facile approach to reduce, exfoliate, and tear graphene oxide (GO) sheets, which can be used to produce graphene quantum dots. The prepared GO sheets were easily exfoliated and teared under UV and microwave (MW) irradiation in the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs). Results show that the GO sheets can be reduced/fragments by MW depending on the irradiation power. We found that; GO sheets are partially reduced under MW irradiation with a power of 90 W and crushed to nanosheets by increasing the MW power up to 450 W. In addition, the photoluminescence peak is broadened, confirming a decrease in the size of GO sheets. The optical bandgap of the nanosheets was measured about 3 eV in agreement with the results of the tight-binding calculation. In addition, HR-TEM results show the size of prepared nanosheets is about 20 nm, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy shows a bond formation between residual TiO2 NPs and the prepared nanosheets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
TiO2 nanoparticles
Microwave

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)-(c) HRTEM images of the prepared nanosheets in GO/TiO2-450 at the scale of 200,100 and 20 nm, and (d) XRD patterns of the prepared samples.
FTIR spectra of the GO, and treated samples by MW.
(a) Raman spectra, and (b) D/G ratio for GO and prepared samples from Raman spectra.

(a) UV–vis spectroscopy and Tauc plot of them for GO, and prepared samples. (b) PL spectra under 325 nm excitation wavelength, and (c)-(e) deconvoluted PL peaks for GO/TiO2-90, GO/TiO2-270, and GO/TiO2-450.
(a)-(h) DOS of graphene nanosheets calculated from the tight-binding model.
Bandgap energy for the calculated graphene nanosheets with different number of aromatic rings.
(a) XPS survey spectra, high-resolution XPS core level spectra for (b)-(e) C 1 s and (e)-(f) Ti 2p for the prepared materials.
Schematic illustration of the describing mechanism for exfoliation and tearing the GO sheets by using the photocatalytic property of the TiO2 NPs.
Relative surface area of decomposed C 1 s peaks from XPS results.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted exfoliation and tearing of graphene oxide in the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Low Dimensional Materials Research Centre (LDMRC), Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Malaya, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Low Dimensional Materials Research Centre (LDMRC), Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Malaya, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we present a facile approach to reduce, exfoliate, and tear graphene oxide (GO) sheets, which can be used to produce graphene quantum dots. The prepared GO sheets were easily exfoliated and teared under UV and microwave (MW) irradiation in the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs). Results show that the GO sheets can be reduced/fragments by MW depending on the irradiation power. We found that; GO sheets are partially reduced under MW irradiation with a power of 90 W and crushed to nanosheets by increasing the MW power up to 450 W. In addition, the photoluminescence peak is broadened, confirming a decrease in the size of GO sheets. The optical bandgap of the nanosheets was measured about 3 eV in agreement with the results of the tight-binding calculation. In addition, HR-TEM results show the size of prepared nanosheets is about 20 nm, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy shows a bond formation between residual TiO2 NPs and the prepared nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and its derivatives have attracted remarkable attention due to promising properties for applications in nanotechnology [1–5]. Graphene derivatives include hydrogenated graphene (graphane), fluorinated graphene (fluorographene), oxidized graphene (graphene oxide) and etc., which are produced by doping the pristine graphene [6]. In addition, tailoring the graphene gives other derivatives such as quantum dots, quantum anti dots, ribbons, and graphene meshes [7,8]. Graphene-based materials can be applied in energy harvesting and storage devices, electrochemistry, electronics and optoelectronics, solar cells, nanosensors, biosensors, and water purification [9–11]. Doping the graphene materials with NPs and surface functional groups can modify their properties according to the desired particular application [12–14]. Many applications for graphene derivatives have been proposed [15,16], and graphene property was engineered by sheet tailoring, doping, and twisting [17]. However, graphene has a zero bandgap, which is a hindrance for nanoelectronic and optoelectronic applications [8,18]. In addition, optical luminescence is not observed in graphene due to its zero bandgap [19].

There are several reports on the bandgap opening in graphene by doping, functionalizing, tailoring, and frustrating in a heterostructure [8,20,21]. Unzipping the graphene sheets converts them to ribbons/graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which induce quantum confinement on charge carriers and can create a bandgap due to edge effects [22,23]. GQDs show interesting properties such as low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility, stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, high specific surface area, high mobility, and tunable bandgap [24–26]. GQDs are graphene nanosheets with the lateral size smaller than 20 nm, and their properties are dependent on the sheet size [27,28]. There are several reports for unzipping and tailoring graphene-based materials [29–31]. Two main approaches have been used to synthesize GQDs, including unzipping the graphene sheets and chemical growth from carbon sources [32–41]. For instance, Zhongxu and Xiang et al. have produced GQDs by using the starch as a precursor [42,43]. In other work, Shixiong et al. presented a facile one-step hydrothermal route for the preparation of GQDs coupled TiO2 (TiO2/GQDs) [44]. In addition, a green and straightforward pyrolysis method was presented for the synthesis of GQDs by using ethylene glycol as a carbon source by Ran et al. [45].

The microwave-assisted technique has been widely used to synthesis nanostructures, including quantum dots and NPs [46–49]. Microwave (MW) irradiation can reduce processing time and improve the purity of the prepared nanostructures. MW is an energy source that typically induces ultrasound, pressure, mechanical activation, and plasma [36,50,51]. As reported in the literature, MW irradiation enhances the efficiency of the chemical reaction by hotspot formation, fast heating, charge and spin polarization [52,53]. MW irradiation heats homogenously entire of the reacting solution and provides the necessary condition uniformly for nucleation centers [52–54]. Previously reported that MW reduce the graphene oxide (GO), and an MW pyrolysis method has been developed to prepare the GQDs [55–58]. For instance, Li et al. reported a facile MW-assisted method to make two-color GQDs with cleaving of GO under acid conditions [59]. In other work, Shan et al. reported a new approach to synthesis the nitrogen-doped carbon dots by MW irradiation to citric acid and ethylenediamine [60]. In addition, Hai Yen et al. reported a different method to produce nitrogen-doped GQDs by using the MW with different levels of power and durations, from citric acid and urea [61]. However, the mentioned methods usually require hazard materials; need several steps, high temperatures, and strong oxidizing agent.

In this paper, we report a facile method to reduce, tear, and exfoliate the GO sheets by sequential MW and UV irradiation. The photocatalytic effect of titanium dioxide (TiO2) NPs under UV irradiation bonds the NPs on the GO, and the MW irradiation cut the sheets. The structural morphology and the size of the nanosheets were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). In addition, the prepared samples were characterized by UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). We found that the power of MW radiation influences the efficiency of GO reducing and tearing.

Graphite oxide was synthesized by oxidation of natural graphite powder (45 μm, Sigma-Aldrich) and then was exfoliated by ultra-sonication as reported in our previous works [55,62]. TiO2 NPs were produced by the arc-discharge method in deionized (DI) water. In the next step, 10 ml of GO solution (1 mg/ml) was dissolved in 150 ml of DI water, and 0.5 mg of the prepared TiO2 NPs was gradually added to the aqueous solution. Then, the aqueous solution was ultrasonicated (40 kHz and power of 100 W) for 10 min. Afterward, the aqueous solution was exposed to the UV-C irradiation for 5 min by MW irradiation at a power of 90, 270, and 450 W for 90 min (we labeled these samples as GO/TiO2-90, GO/TiO2-270, and GO/TiO2-450). Herein UV-C irradiation was used to activate the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 NPs, and MW irradiation was used to affect the subsequent reaction by ultrasound, pressure, mechanical activation. Eventually, to separate the residual TiO2 NPs, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min.

The UV–vis spectra were recorded at room temperature in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm by Analytik Jena Sotax Specord 50 Plus UV–vis. The photoluminescence and Raman spectra of the samples were recorded at room temperature by an InVia Raman microscope equipped with a He-Cd laser at 325 nm excitation wavelength. FT-IR spectra of the prepared samples were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 FT-IR Spectrometer. The chemical compositions and bonding configurations of the samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantera II). HRTEM images were taken with a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope operating at 200 kV. XRD analysis has been performed by a powder diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å).

The electronic properties of graphene nanosheets were studied using the tight-binding model. Our model consists of an aromatic carbon ring that can be the smallest nanosheet; at each step, the new aromatic ring was added to the previous aromatic ring. To calculate the Hamiltonian of the system, we use the following equation [63]:
(1)
H = - t
∑
< i , j > , σ
a
i , σ
†
b
j , σ
-
t ~
∑
< i , j > , σ
a
i , σ
†
a
j , σ
+
b
i , σ
†
b
j , σ
+ H . c .
The hopping parameter was set to 3.2 eV, then the density of states (DOS) were calculated for nanosheets with different sizes, and their bandgaps were extracted from DOS.

The structural morphology, size, and crystal structure of the prepared nanosheets were investigated by the use of HRTEM images. Fig. 1 (a)-(c) show the HRTEM images of the GO/TiO2-450 nanosheets in different scales with a lattice parameter of 0.241 nm, similar to the hexagonal pattern of graphene with the (0001) lattice fringes [64]. In Fig. 1 (c) the lattice spacing of the nanosheets is 0.241 nm, smaller than the (002) interlayer spacing of bulk graphite (0.334 nm). The hexagonal arrangement of the diffraction spots in the FFT images in the inset of Fig. 1 (c) indicates the high crystallinity of the nanosheets. However, as shown in Fig. 1 (d), the XRD analysis shows a broadened peak at about 25.6° corresponding to the (002) plane with d spacing of 0.33 nm. The peak at 10.5° also attributed to (001) plane, which is disappeared by further MW irradiation due to GO reduction and exfoliation. This is in agreement with the previous results for graphene nanosheets [65].

The FTIR analysis was used to determine the functional groups on the prepared sheets. Fig. 2 illustrates the FTIR spectra of the GO and the prepared samples. In a typical spectrum of GO, the stretching vibration of O–H, C–H, C = O, and C-O appears in the wavenumbers at ~ 3440, 2930, 1740, and 1138 cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, two peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 correspond to C = C, and C–C in alkene and alkane groups [66]. In addition, a peak centered at 600–700 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of the Ti-O [67,68]. The GO/TiO2 peaks gradually decreased and disappeared with increasing the power of MW irradiation, suggesting GO reducing and degrading in GO/TiO2-450 samples while for GO/TiO2-90 and GO/TiO2-270 signifying reducing and anchoring TiO2 on sheets.

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to characterize the crystalline quality of the prepared samples. As shown the inset of Fig. 3 (a), two peaks are observed at 1331 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 are assigned to the disordered sp3 carbon (D-band) and well-ordered graphite (G-band), respectively [69,70]. Results show that after exfoliating and tearing the GO, a shift in D-band and G-band is observed by increasing the MW power. In addition, Fig. 3 (b) exhibits the D/G peak ratio, which rises from 0.84 to 0.85 by enhancing MW power. The D to G line ratio is attributed to defects typically at the edges of GO and graphite platelets. Increasing the D/G peak ratio confirms the exfoliation and tearing of the GO sheets [71]. Moreover, in Fig. 3 (a) the specific vibration modes are observed at around 150 (E1g), 418 (B1g), and 609 cm−1 (overtone of Eg), indicating the presence of the anatase phase in the prepared samples [72]. It is observed that the peaks intensity decreases by enhancing the MW power, and a shift in B1g is observed from 418 to 425 and then 431 cm−1 for GO/TiO2-270 and GO/TiO2-450, respectively. In addition, the peak at ~ 254 cm−1 corresponds to the Ti-O-C band [73], and the
I
T i - O - C
I G
ratio decreases from 0.96 in GO/TiO2-90 to 0.92 and 0.69 by rising the power of MW to 270, and 450 W, respectively. These can be attributed to the partial reduction and then tearing of GO sheets due to MW irradiation up to 450 W, which releases the TiO2 NPs after degrading the GO.

The optical properties of the prepared samples were investigated by UV–vis spectra and PL spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectra of the prepared aqueous solution have been presented in Fig. 4 (a). There is an absorption peak at ~ 230 nm for the GO solution, which corresponds to the π → π* transition. Also, there is a shoulder at ~ 300 nm for GO solutions, which can be attributed to the n → π* transition [74]. As shown for GO/TiO2-90 sample, the peak intensity decreases, and the shoulder disappears due to GO reduction. However, for GO/TiO2-270 and GO/TiO2-450, the peak intensity decreases gradually together with a red-shifted from 336 to 323 nm, indicating the partial reducing and degrading of the GO. The peak at ~ 335 nm is assigned to the GO nanosheets that shows a blue shift from 335 nm to 324 nm by increasing the power of MW. However, the shift in the absorption band edge to shorter wavelengths is attributed to the decrease in the size of sheets [75]. The inset in Fig. 4 (a) shows the Tauc plot of the GO and the prepared samples. Results show that the bandgap of GO is ~ 3.41 eV, which decreases to 2.75 eV for GO/TiO2-90, indicating the partial reduction of GO. In addition, the bandgap increases to 2.94 eV and 3.02 eV for GO/TiO2-270, GO/TiO2-450, respectively. This can be attributed to the exfoliating and tearing of the GO sheets besides linkage between TiO2 NPs and the sheets. According to shrinkage of TiO2 NPs peaks in Raman spectra, one can conclude that exfoliating and tearing the GO sheets is the main reason for the bandgap increasing.

PL spectroscopy of the nanosheets in Fig. 4 (b) shows the luminescence peak at around 600 nm for the prepared samples [76]. The results show a blue shift from 610 to 594 nm due to increas in the power of irradiated MW, which confirms previously reported results [77]. The inset of Fig. 4 (b) shows the nanosheets luminescence photograph under UV with a wavelength of 365 nm. To show the change in PL peaks, we deconvoluted them, as shown in Fig. 4 (c)-(e). PL emission can be due to disorder-induced localized states and confined cluster states in GO and RGO, respectively [78]. In addition, the emission peak in PL spectra shows a blue shift due to GO reduction and exfoliation [79]. Therefore, emission peaks at about 540, 595, and 665 nm due to the π → π*, n → π* transitions in nanosheets (Fig. 4 (c)-(e)) can be ascribed to teared sheets, reduced GO and GO, respectively [79]. As shown, the peak intensity at about 540 nm increases significantly by enhancing the power of MW irradiation showing tearing the sheets.

We can estimate the exciton radius from the bandgap in nanosheets by taking into account quantum confinement and Coulomb attraction between created electron and hole as follows [80]:
(2)
E
g
( n s h )
=
E
g
( b u l k )

E
( w e l l )
+
E
( C o u l o m b )

Since
E
( w e l l )
=
h
2
8 μ
r
2
and
E
( C o u l o m b )
=
- 1.8
e
2
4
ε 0
ε r
.
we arrive at:
(3)
E
g
( n s h )
=
E
g
( b u l k )
+
h
2
8 μ
r
2

1.8

4
ε 0
ε r

In this equation,
E
g
( b u l k )
(0.046 eV) is the bandgap of bulk graphite [81],
μ =
m e
m h
m e
+
m h
=
0.05926 m0 is the reduced mass of the exciton in nanosheets. Also, m0 , me , and mh , are the free electron mass, the effective mass of electron and hole, respectively. ε is the dielectric constant of graphite (~15), and ε0 is the vacuum permittivity constant. According to the bandgap results, the exciton radius is estimated as 1.44 nm in nanosheets. Radius dependency also studied by the tight-binding model. Fig. 5 shows the DOS of graphene nanosheets. As shown, by increasing the number of aromatic rings, the DOS has changed, and the bandgap decreases from 5.42 to 0 eV. For one aromatic ring, the bandgap is calculated as 5.42 eV. Fig. 6 shows the calculated bandgap as a function of the ring number. According to the measured bandgaps, the size of graphene nanosheets predicted smaller than 20 nm, which is in agreement with previous results.

The chemical analysis of the prepared samples was carried out by the XPS spectra. Fig. 7 (a) shows the XPS survey spectra of the GO and the prepared samples. Two peaks are observed at 284.5 eV and 532 eV, which are corresponded to core C1s and O1s, respectively [14]. As shown in Fig. 7 (b)-(e), the C1s peak of the GO and prepared samples can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.5, 285.7, 286.9, and 288.3 eV. These peaks are assigned to two types of carbon: graphitic carbon (C = C and C–C), and oxygenated carbon (C = O and O-C = O). The results are summarized in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, C = C bond peak intensity increases at first and then decreases by raising the power of MW irradiation, while for C–C bond and O-C = O bonds the intensity of peaks reduces. This is attributed to the GO partial reducing and then tearing, which produced nanosheets. The residual TiO2 NPs on the nanosheets show Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 7 (e)-(f)) with two main peaks centered at 459.35 and 465.11 eV, which are attributed to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, respectively. The absence of Ti-C signal at 281 eV suggest that carbon does not have considerable direct bond with Ti. However, the additional weak peaks around the Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 peaks (see Table 1) are accorded with the Ti+3 that authenticate the strong adhesion of NPs on the nanosheets through surface defects on TiO2 NPs [82].

Degrading and reduction of GO by TiO2 NPs can be explained by the following proposed mechanism. Fig. 8 shows the main preparation process of graphene nanosheets. MW irradiation energy and its electromagnetic field decrease the activation energy of reactions by a polar transition state [83]. This transition can change the electron cloud distribution, bond length and/or angle, which activate the reaction. Under UV-C and MW irradiation, the photoexcited electrons of the TiO2 NPs were injected into the GO sheets, which identified as good electron acceptors [84]. Owing to the high mobility of the electrons in the sheets, the injected electrons can move throughout the sheet by reducing it. Therefore, the holes in the excited TiO2 NPs could effectively produce OH● radicals for the local oxidation and, afterward, the local reduction and degradation of the GO sheets. During this process, GO sheets automatically can reduce and then break down into nanosheets [84,85].

We presented an MW-assisted method for exfoliating and tearing the GO sheets by using the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 NPs. UV–Vis results show that GO is reduced at first and then teared by raising the power of MW irradiation. PL results show decreases in the diameters of the nanosheets by increasing the power of MW irradiation as well as optical bandgap. The Raman and XPS analyses show a linkage between the TiO2 NPs and GO which tears the sheets by MW irradiation. In addition, HR-TEM results confirm the production of nanosheets by showing their lattice parameter as 2.41 Å and diameters of them in the range of 2–20 nm.

Hadi Rasuli: Writing - original draft. Reza Rasuli: Validation, Data curation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Mahdi Alizadeh: Investigation, Conceptualization. Goh BoonTong: Formal analysis, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the University of Zanjan for the financial supports of this work. Authors is also grateful to the Iranian National Science Foundation (INSF) for the support.
